subject: TOEFL iBT
description: Deep detail for each TOEFL skill area, supporting error analysis, personalized feedback, and targeted practice.

reading.name: Reading Comprehension
reading.description: Academic reading passages with 10 question types

skills.name: Vocabulary and Reference
skills.summary: Understand word meanings in context and identify pronoun references.
keyConcepts: Context clues: definition, example, contrast, restatement
Pronoun reference: what noun a pronoun refers to
Word families: recognizing root words, prefixes, suffixes
Academic vocabulary: discipline-specific terms
commonErrors: Choosing dictionary definition instead of context-appropriate meaning
Missing that pronouns can refer to entire phrases, not just single nouns
Confusing similar-looking words (affect/effect, cite/site/sight)
Overlooking that 'it' may refer to an idea, not a concrete object
examTips: For vocabulary: the correct answer is always a synonym in context
For reference: the pronoun and its referent always match in number
Read the sentence before AND after the pronoun to find the referent
socraticPrompts: What clues in the surrounding sentences help you understand this word?
If 'they' refers to researchers, why can't it refer to the experiments?
How would the meaning change if we used a different word here?

skills.name: Factual Information
skills.summary: Identify explicitly stated information and find exceptions.
keyConcepts: Scanning vs. close reading
Paraphrase recognition
Signal words for exceptions (however, although, except)
Distinguishing main points from details
commonErrors: Choosing answers that are true but not in the passage
Missing the 'NOT' or 'EXCEPT' in negative fact questions
Selecting a detail when the question asks for main point
examTips: For negative fact questions: three answers are true, one is false
The correct answer may be a paraphrase, not exact words
socraticPrompts: Is this information directly stated or implied? How can you tell?
Why are the other three options correct? Find evidence for each.

skills.name: Inference and Rhetorical Purpose
skills.summary: Draw logical conclusions and understand why the author includes information.
keyConcepts: Strong vs. weak inference: what MUST be true vs. MIGHT be true
Author's purpose: explain, contrast, illustrate, qualify
Transition words as purpose signals
Reading between the lines without adding outside knowledge
commonErrors: Making leaps beyond what the text logically supports
Confusing inference with factual information
Misreading the author's tone (sarcasm, skepticism, support)
examTips: The correct inference is always directly supported by the text
Ask: 'Why does the author mention X?' Look at surrounding sentences
socraticPrompts: What must be true based on these sentences? What could be true but isn't proven?
If the author wanted to give an example, what words would they use?

skills.name: Sentence Simplification
skills.summary: Choose the sentence that best captures the essential meaning of a complex sentence.
keyConcepts: Identifying main idea vs. supporting details
Recognizing logical relationships (cause-effect, contrast, condition)
Eliminating less important modifiers
Preserving the original meaning while reducing length
commonErrors: Omitting a key logical relationship (e.g., dropping 'although')
Including minor details that change the emphasis
Choosing an answer that changes the meaning
examTips: The correct answer preserves ALL essential information
Check that the logical relationship (however, because, etc.) is maintained

skills.name: Insert Text
skills.summary: Determine where a given sentence best fits in the passage.
keyConcepts: Transition and linking words
Pronoun reference across sentences
Logical flow of ideas
Sentence connectors (this, such, these, another)
commonErrors: Not checking pronoun references (inserted sentence has 'this idea' but previous sentence has no idea)
Breaking logical connections between sentences
Missing that the inserted sentence should connect to both previous and following sentences
examTips: Check the sentence BEFORE and AFTER each insertion point
Look for pronoun references and transition words that must connect

skills.name: Prose Summary
skills.summary: Select the three most important ideas that best summarize the passage.
keyConcepts: Major ideas vs. supporting details
Hierarchy of information in academic texts
Distinguishing main points from examples
Recognizing the passage's organizational structure
commonErrors: Choosing minor details or examples
Selecting statements not in the passage
Missing that all three correct answers must be major points
examTips: The correct answers cover the passage's main structure
If an answer is a specific example, it's probably wrong

skills.name: Fill in a Table
skills.summary: Categorize information from the passage into a table.
keyConcepts: Categorization and classification
Comparing and contrasting theories/views
Identifying which category each detail belongs to
Recognizing when information applies to multiple categories
commonErrors: Misclassifying information
Including information not in the passage
Missing that some categories may have more than one correct answer

listening.name: Listening Comprehension
listening.description: Academic lectures and conversations with comprehension questions

skills.name: Lecture: Main Idea and Purpose
skills.summary: Identify the overall topic and why the speaker is discussing it.
keyConcepts: Lecture openings: thesis statements and previews
Signal phrases: 'Today we'll discuss...', 'My focus today is...'
Distinguishing topic from purpose
Recognizing when speakers digress and return
commonErrors: Choosing a detail instead of the main idea
Confusing the topic (what) with the purpose (why)
Missing that the professor may be responding to student questions
examTips: The main idea is usually stated in the first 30 seconds
If you're not sure, listen for what keeps coming up

skills.name: Lecture: Details and Examples
skills.summary: Understand specific information and why examples are given.
keyConcepts: Signal words: 'for example', 'for instance', 'such as'
Purpose of examples: illustrate, clarify, support
Details that support main points vs. tangential information
Numbers, dates, names as potential detail questions
commonErrors: Forgetting that the example's purpose matters more than the example itself
Missing that a detail question may ask about WHY something was mentioned
socraticPrompts: Why did the professor mention this specific experiment?
What point was this example supporting?

skills.name: Lecture: Attitude and Stance
skills.summary: Understand the speaker's opinion, certainty, or attitude.
keyConcepts: Tone indicators: 'interestingly', 'surprisingly', 'unfortunately'
Hesitation and uncertainty markers
Comparing and contrasting theories: the professor may favor one
Qualifying language: 'might', 'could', 'possibly' vs. 'definitely'
commonErrors: Attributing a personal opinion when the professor is just reporting
Missing subtle disagreement with a cited theory
Confusing uncertainty about facts with uncertainty about interpretation
examTips: Listen for adjectives: 'a brilliant study' vs. 'a controversial study'
The professor's true attitude often comes at the end of a discussion

skills.name: Lecture: Organization and Relationship
skills.summary: Understand how ideas are organized and how they relate.
keyConcepts: Organizational patterns: compare-contrast, cause-effect, problem-solution
Transition words signaling relationships
How different theories or viewpoints relate
The overall structure of the lecture
commonErrors: Missing that two ideas are being compared
Not recognizing when the speaker is contrasting their view with others

skills.name: Lecture: Inference and Prediction
skills.summary: Draw conclusions and predict what might happen next.
keyConcepts: Implied meaning vs. stated meaning
What the professor assumes the student already knows
Predicting next steps in a process
Understanding the professor's pedagogical purpose
commonErrors: Making inferences that require outside knowledge
Predicting something that contradicts what was said

skills.name: Conversation: Purpose and Main Idea
skills.summary: Understand why students talk to professors or staff.
keyConcepts: Common purposes: get information, resolve an issue, ask for help
Opening lines: 'I'm here because...', 'I needed to ask about...'
The stated purpose vs. the real need
Multiple purposes in one conversation
commonErrors: Stopping at the stated purpose when there's a deeper need
Missing that the conversation might have two purposes

skills.name: Conversation: Details and Implication
skills.summary: Understand specific information and what it means.
keyConcepts: Solutions to problems
What the student must do next
Implications of what the professor says
Understanding university procedures
commonErrors: Missing that the professor's suggestion implies the student must do something
Not connecting a detail to the larger situation

skills.name: Conversation: Attitude and Stance
skills.summary: Understand how speakers feel about the topic or each other.
keyConcepts: Tone: helpful, frustrated, apologetic, encouraging
Politeness strategies and their meaning
Hesitation as a signal of uncertainty or discomfort
Agreement and disagreement signals
commonErrors: Missing that 'I guess I could...' means reluctant agreement, not enthusiasm
Not recognizing when a professor is being politely firm

skills.name: Independent: Personal Preference
skills.summary: Express and support a personal opinion on a familiar topic.
keyConcepts: Clear thesis: state your preference immediately
Organization: reason 1 → example/explanation, reason 2 → example/explanation
Transition words: first, also, for example
Conclusion: brief restatement
commonErrors: Not stating opinion clearly at the beginning
Vague examples without specific details
Running out of time before giving both reasons
Memorized responses that don't fit the question
examTips: Use the 15 seconds to plan: pick two clear reasons
Be specific: 'my brother' not 'someone I know'
It's okay to pause briefly between ideas
socraticPrompts: Why do you prefer that? What's the main reason?
Can you give a specific example of when that happened?
What would you say if you had only 30 seconds?

skills.name: Independent: Choice Between Two
skills.summary: Choose between two options and explain your choice.
keyConcepts: Acknowledge both sides briefly, then commit
Comparison language: more important, better because
Specific reasons why one option outweighs the other
Examples that illustrate the comparison
commonErrors: Discussing both options equally without choosing
Changing opinion mid-response
Not explaining why the other option is weaker
examTips: It's okay to say 'both have benefits, but I prefer X because...'
The comparison itself can be your structure

skills.name: Integrated: Campus Announcement
skills.summary: Read an announcement, listen to a conversation, then summarize the opinion.
keyConcepts: Reading: identify the proposal/change and two reasons
Listening: identify the speaker's opinion (agree/disagree) and reasons
Integration: connect the listening's reasons to the reading's points
Structure: opinion → reason 1 (with reading/listening link) → reason 2
commonErrors: Describing the reading and listening separately without connecting them
Missing that the listening reasons directly respond to reading reasons
Reporting the conversation but forgetting the reading context
Stating the opinion but not the supporting reasons
examTips: Your response should show HOW the listening relates to the reading
The speaker's reasons will directly address the reading's points

skills.name: Integrated: Academic Lecture
skills.summary: Read a short passage, listen to a lecture, then summarize key points.
keyConcepts: Reading: general concept or theory
Listening: examples or applications of the concept
Structure: concept → example 1 (explain how it illustrates) → example 2
Explicit connections: 'The professor illustrates this with...'
commonErrors: Describing the examples without linking to the concept
Giving equal detail to both, missing the hierarchy
Forgetting to state the concept clearly at the beginning
examTips: The reading gives you the framework; the listening gives the content
Each listening example should be connected back to the reading concept

skills.name: Integrated Writing
skills.summary: Read a passage, listen to a lecture, then write summarizing the relationship.
keyConcepts: Reading: presents a theory/argument with 3 main points
Listening: challenges/casts doubt on each point (usually)
Structure: introduction (reading's claim, lecture's response) → 3 body paragraphs
Each body paragraph: lecture's point + how it challenges reading
commonErrors: Summarizing reading and listening separately without showing the relationship
Missing that the lecture usually OPPOSES the reading
Adding personal opinion (not allowed in integrated task)
Not covering all three points from the lecture
examTips: The relationship is almost always 'the lecture challenges the reading'
Use contrast language: however, in contrast, on the other hand
150-225 words is the sweet spot
socraticPrompts: How does the lecture's first point respond to the reading?
Does the lecture agree, disagree, or partially agree? How do you know?

skills.name: Independent Writing
skills.summary: Write an essay expressing and supporting an opinion on a given topic.
keyConcepts: Thesis: clear position stated in introduction
Body paragraphs: each with a topic sentence and specific examples/reasons
Organization: 4-5 paragraphs typical
Development: specific, detailed examples, not vague generalities
commonErrors: Vague examples ('for example, in many countries...')
Off-topic paragraphs
Not enough development (one sentence per reason)
Memorized templates that don't fit the prompt
examTips: Quality over quantity: 300-350 well-developed words > 500 rambling words
Use personal experience as evidence
Leave 3-5 minutes to review for errors
socraticPrompts: Can you make that example more specific? When did this happen?
How does this reason connect back to your main argument?
What would someone who disagrees say? How would you respond?
